column1: Textual Sources and Dates
Baal Cycle: KTU 1.1–1.6, Ugarit, c. 1400–1200 BCE58
Enuma Elish: Akkadian, c. 1100–700 BCE (composition debated; earliest manuscripts 9th c. BCE)67910
Song of Ullikummi / Kumarbi Cycle: Hurrian-Hittite, c. 1400–1200 BCE8
Rig Veda: Sanskrit, India, c. 1200–1000 BCE
Greek Theogony: Hesiod, c. 700 BCE
Norse Edda: Icelandic, c. 13th c. CE (oral traditions much earlier)
Egyptian Pyramid Texts/Osiris: c. 2300 BCE
Sumerian Dumuzi/Inanna: c. 2100 BCE
Avesta (Zoroastrian): Iran, c. 1000–600 BCE
Hebrew Bible: Genesis, Psalms, Isaiah, Job, 1 Kings, Judges, Daniel (various, c. 10th–2nd c. BCE)
Slavic, Baltic, and other Eurasian oral traditions: Various, recorded in medieval or modern times but often much older in origin

Here is a reconstructed, comparative timeline table of the core mythic narrative shared across the Baal Cycle, Enuma Elish, Proto-Indo-European (PIE) myths, Hellenic cycles, Gilgamesh cycles, and biblical stories, arranged in a generalized order of events. This synthesis draws on comparative mythology scholarship to align motifs and episodes from these traditions, with textual sources and approximate composition dates included.
Notes on Reconstruction
This table synthesizes shared motifs and episodes common to these mythic traditions, recognizing that each tradition has unique elements and theological emphases.
The ordering reflects a generalized mythic "life cycle" of a storm/sky god or culture hero: from primordial chaos, through combat and kingship, death and resurrection, to cosmic order and fertility.
Some myths (e.g., Enuma Elish) emphasize creation and kingship without death/resurrection; others (Baal Cycle, Gilgamesh, biblical texts) incorporate death and renewal motifs.
Female divine figures play crucial roles in mourning and restoration in several traditions (Anat, Isis, Demeter, Mary).
The Gilgamesh cycle adds the human quest for immortality and reflections on mortality, enriching the mythic narrative with existential themes.
Biblical narratives often polemicize or reinterpret these motifs to emphasize monotheism and covenantal theology.
